title: David Hume
summary: The Scottish philosopher who pushed Enlightenment empiricism to its radical conclusions — showing that reason alone cannot prove causation, establish the self, or justify belief in miracles — and who produced the most systematic challenge to religious belief and rationalist philosophy that the Western tradition has faced.
hook: Hume took the Enlightenment's commitment to experience over authority and followed it to conclusions the Enlightenment found uncomfortable. If we can only know what experience gives us, reason cannot prove that the sun will rise tomorrow, that the self exists, or that God is real. Kant said Hume woke him from his 'dogmatic slumber.' The slumber was comfortable. Hume was not.

beginner.theFigure:
David Hume was a Scottish philosopher of the eighteenth century. He wanted to apply scientific methods to the human mind. He wanted to discover the laws governing how we form beliefs, make decisions, and feel emotions — without assuming anything beyond what experience actually shows.

What he found was more radical than he expected. Following empiricism's logic, he concluded we cannot rationally justify some basic beliefs: that causes produce effects, that the self exists, that the future resembles the past, or that religious belief is rationally grounded. He published these conclusions calmly. The Enlightenment has been wrestling with them ever since.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Hume grew up in the Scottish Enlightenment — a world of thinkers applying scientific methods to human affairs. The Enlightenment project was to replace superstition with reason. Hume was its most rigorous practitioner and its most uncomfortable critic. He showed that reason, taken seriously, leads to conclusions that rational people find deeply unsettling.

The religious context matters. Hume lived in a society where religion was assumed as the foundation of public life. His arguments against miracles, against the design argument for God's existence, and against rational grounds for religious belief were published carefully — some posthumously. He died a non-believer in a believing society. He died calmly, which his contemporaries found remarkable and troubling.
beginner.centralIdea:
Hume's most important argument is about causation. We see the sun rise every morning and conclude that the sun must rise tomorrow. We see a billiard ball strike another and conclude that the first must have caused the second to move. But what justifies these conclusions?

Hume's answer: nothing rational does. Our belief that causes produce effects is based on habit, not reason. We have always seen it work this way, so we expect it to continue. But no logical argument can prove it will. This argument — known as the problem of induction — is one of philosophy's genuinely unresolved problems. We run our lives and our sciences on inductive inference; we cannot prove it is reliable.

The same radical empiricism undermined the self. When Hume looked into his own mind, he could not find a 'self' — only a bundle of perceptions, following one another rapidly. There is no continuous entity behind experience; there is only experience itself.
beginner.howSpread:
Hume's influence spread through his most careful readers — even those who disagreed with him. Kant famously said that Hume woke him from his 'dogmatic slumber' and set him to work on the Critique of Pure Reason. That work was largely an attempt to answer Hume's challenge. The Scottish Common Sense school (Reid, Beattie) argued that common sense must be our starting point rather than radical skepticism.

In ethics, Hume's argument that 'reason is the slave of the passions' became foundational. Reason alone cannot motivate action; only desires and emotions can. This grounded a tradition that derives ethics from sentiment rather than pure reason.
beginner.contestedInheritance: Hume's inheritance is contested on whether his skeptical arguments are devastating or manageable. Philosophers who think them devastating follow his conclusions wherever they lead. Philosophers who think them manageable — including Kant and most analytic philosophers after him — argue we can rescue knowledge by specifying the conditions under which it is possible. Whether Hume is best read as a radical skeptic or as a naturalist who grounds knowledge in human psychology rather than pure reason remains genuinely debated.

general.theFigure:
Barry Stroud's Hume — the definitive analytic account — argues that Hume's skepticism is more radical than is usually acknowledged. Hume does not merely show that we cannot rationally prove causation; he shows that we cannot rationally justify our most basic ways of thinking about the world. The response that 'well, we just have to get on with our lives' is psychologically accurate but philosophically unsatisfying: it concedes the skeptical point while declining to engage with it.

Annette Baier's reading of Hume — emphasizing the ethics of the Enquiries and the Treatise's account of the 'calm passions' — presents a Hume less concerned with skeptical demolition and more interested in understanding the emotional and social foundations of moral life. This Hume is a forerunner of sentimentalism in ethics and of what would now be called moral psychology. Baier's Hume is not trying to destroy knowledge but to understand how human beings actually form beliefs and make moral judgments.
general.worldOfIdeas:
The Enlightenment's relationship to religion was more varied than its critics often acknowledge. Newton was deeply religious; Locke accepted Christianity while arguing for toleration; Voltaire was deist rather than atheist. Hume was exceptional in his philosophical seriousness about the arguments against religious belief. His Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion — published posthumously in 1779 — remains the most rigorous philosophical dissection of the design argument for God's existence. His essay 'Of Miracles' — arguing that testimony for miracles can never outweigh the evidence from the regularity of nature — remains the most careful philosophical analysis of religious testimony.

The Dialogues' three characters — Cleanthes (who defends the design argument), Philo (who attacks it), and Demea (who defends a priori religion) — reach no definitive conclusion. The identity of Hume's own position is contested. Most careful readers treat him as Philo: skeptical about natural theology, dismissive of enthusiasm, probably atheist in private while cautious in public.
general.centralIdea:
The problem of induction, which Hume identified, has proved extraordinarily difficult to solve. The logical positivists in the early twentieth century tried to dissolve it by arguing that inductive reasoning is just the definition of rationality — we have decided that expecting the future to resemble the past is what 'rational' means. This response is unsatisfying because it concedes that induction is not justified by anything outside itself. Karl Popper tried to solve the problem by replacing induction with falsification — science proceeds not by accumulating confirming instances but by eliminating false theories. But Popper's account faces its own difficulties, and the problem of induction remains genuinely unsolved.

Popper's falsificationism — science proceeds not by confirming but by eliminating false theories — faces its own difficulties. The Bayesian response — induction is rational given prior probabilities — requires justifying the priors. The problem remains one of the most important unsolved problems in epistemology, with direct implications for the rational foundations of science.
general.howSpread: Hume's influence on Adam Smith — his closest friend — is understudied but significant. The Theory of Moral Sentiments and The Wealth of Nations share with Hume's work the project of understanding human social behavior through observation of actual human psychology rather than idealized rational-agent models. Smith's 'impartial spectator' as the mechanism of moral judgment has Humean roots: it grounds morality in sympathy and social psychology rather than reason. The Scottish Enlightenment's empirical approach to human affairs, which Hume exemplified, runs through Smith's economics.
general.contestedInheritance:
The naturalistic interpretation of Hume — developed particularly by Norman Kemp Smith and subsequently by Don Garrett — presents him not as a skeptic who destroys knowledge but as a naturalist who investigates the psychological mechanisms through which humans form beliefs. On this reading, the skeptical arguments are a propadeutic — clearing away bad metaphysics — rather than the final conclusion. What survives Humean skepticism is not reason but human nature: the habits, customs, and sentiments through which we actually navigate the world.

Simon Blackburn's 'quasi-realism' in ethics attempts to vindicate moral realism from within a Humean framework that grounds ethics in sentiment rather than reason. The project is to show that even if moral judgments express attitudes rather than beliefs, the practices of moral reasoning, argument, and progress are not thereby undermined. Quasi-realism has been influential in meta-ethics as a middle ground between full moral realism and expressivism — preserving the form of moral discourse while grounding it in Humean sentiment.

advanced.theFigure:
The question of whether Hume's skepticism is global (undermining all rational justification) or local (undermining specific rationalist pretensions) is the central interpretive debate in Hume scholarship. The global skeptic reading — championed by Stroud — argues that Hume's arguments have no principled stopping point: once you admit that causal inference is unjustified, the same logic applies to everything we believe about the world. The local skeptic reading — associated with Kemp Smith and Garrett — argues that Hume's target was specifically rationalist metaphysics (Descartes, Locke) and that his naturalism provides a positive account of knowledge that survives the critique.

The interpretive debate between 'pyrrhonian' Hume (global skeptic) and 'naturalist' Hume (positive account of knowledge through custom and habit) maps onto the question of what Hume was trying to do. If he was primarily a destroyer — clearing away rationalist metaphysics — then the skeptical arguments are the culmination. If he was primarily a constructive naturalist — building an empirical science of human nature — then the skeptical arguments are propadeutic, clearing space for a positive account of how human minds actually work. Most contemporary scholarship favors the naturalist reading without entirely abandoning the skeptical dimensions.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Hume's Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion presents three characters — Cleanthes (who defends the design argument), Philo (who attacks it), and Demea (who defends a priori religion) — in a dialogue that reaches no definitive conclusion. The identity of Hume's own position — whether he is Philo, whether Philo concedes too much at the end, whether the dialogue has a conclusion — is contested. The most careful readings treat the Dialogues as a genuine dialogue in which no position is fully vindicated, and Hume's personal position as probably that of Philo: skepticism about natural theology, combined with contempt for enthusiasm but perhaps not complete atheism.

Hume's engagement with Newtonian science shaped his project in underappreciated ways. Newton had explained the laws governing physical nature through observation and induction, without claiming to know the underlying causes of gravity. Hume attempted the same thing for human nature: discover the laws governing belief formation and motivation through careful observation, without claiming to know the ultimate metaphysical causes. The 'science of man' was explicitly modeled on the Newtonian approach — which is part of why Hume's skeptical arguments are so problematic for his own project: the Newtonian method he adopted is itself based on induction.

Hume's relationship to religious enthusiasm is more complicated than his reputation as a secular rationalist suggests. He distinguished sharply between 'popular religion' (superstition and enthusiasm, which he despised) and philosophical religion (which he subjected to skeptical analysis). His Natural History of Religion traces religious belief to its psychological origins — fear, hope, the tendency to anthropomorphize — in a move that modern cognitive science of religion has refined but not fundamentally overturned. Hume was not dismissing religion as irrational: he was explaining it as a natural product of the human mind operating under uncertainty, which is a different and more sophisticated critique.
advanced.centralIdea:
Hume's distinction between 'is' and 'ought' — often called the 'is-ought problem' or 'Hume's guillotine' — is the most influential statement of the naturalistic fallacy in the history of ethics. Hume notes that moral arguments typically slide from observations about what is the case to conclusions about what ought to be the case, without justifying the transition. The observation that humans have always lived in families does not by itself justify the conclusion that family structures ought to be maintained. This gap between descriptive and normative claims has been enormously influential in ethics, meta-ethics, and social science methodology.

Hume's bundle theory of personal identity — the claim that the self is not a substantial entity but a bundle of perceptions — has found unexpected resonance in neuroscience and Buddhist philosophy. Neuroscientist Thomas Metzinger argues that the sense of being a unified self is a construction rather than a basic fact, consistent with Hume's analysis. Buddhist no-self doctrine (anatta) makes a parallel claim from a different starting point. The convergence of analytic philosophy, Buddhist philosophy, and neuroscience on this point is one of the more striking examples of cross-cultural philosophical convergence — though whether the convergence is genuine or a product of selective reading is debated.
advanced.howSpread:
The reception of Hume in German philosophy — through Kant and subsequently — is the most consequential channel of his influence. Kant's response, developed in the three Critiques, attempted to show that categories of understanding (including causation) are not derived from experience but are preconditions of it. This 'Copernican revolution' — making the mind's contribution to knowledge central — was Kant's answer to Hume. Whether it succeeds is still debated, and much of subsequent European philosophy has been a negotiation with the Hume-Kant exchange.

Hume's influence on contemporary philosophy of science is less direct but equally significant. The demarcation problem — how to distinguish science from non-science — is partly a response to the problem of induction. If inductive inference cannot be rationally justified, the authority of scientific knowledge rests on something other than its logical structure. Popper's falsificationism, Lakatos's research programs, and Kuhn's paradigms are all attempts to ground scientific authority without claiming that induction is logically valid. Each inherits Hume's problem even as it proposes a different solution.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
Simon Blackburn's 'quasi-realism' in ethics attempts to vindicate moral realism — the view that moral claims can be true — from within a Humean framework that grounds ethics in sentiment rather than reason. The project is to show that even if moral judgments express attitudes rather than beliefs, the practices of moral reasoning, moral argument, and moral progress are not thereby undermined. Quasi-realism has been influential in meta-ethics as a middle ground between full realism and expressivism.

Hume's treatment of religion remains the most contested dimension of his legacy. His Dialogues are deliberately inconclusive; his essays on religion are carefully qualified. Scholars disagree about whether he was an atheist who concealed his views for safety, a genuine agnostic who thought the evidence underdetermined the question, or something more complicated. James Boswell's account of Hume's deathbed cheerfulness — and his refusal to recant — became an iconic moment in the history of secular dying: evidence that a person of philosophical conviction could face death without religious consolation.

foundation.theStory: Hume took the Enlightenment's most fundamental commitment — that knowledge comes from experience rather than authority or tradition — and followed it to conclusions that the Enlightenment itself found uncomfortable. Causation cannot be rationally proven. The self cannot be observed. The future cannot be guaranteed to resemble the past. Religion's evidential basis is insufficient. These conclusions, stated calmly and clearly, reshaped philosophy and remain unresolved.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern how radical Hume's skepticism is (global vs. local skepticism), what his positive view of knowledge is (naturalism as an alternative to skepticism), and whether his ethical sentimentalism is an adequate foundation for moral philosophy. The problem of induction — Hume's most famous contribution to epistemology — has not been solved, and whether it can be is still debated.
foundation.whyItMatters: Hume matters as the philosopher who identified the limits of reason most precisely and who showed that the Enlightenment's project of basing knowledge on reason alone could not be completed. He matters for the history of ethics — grounding morality in sentiment and sympathy rather than reason. And he matters for philosophy of religion — providing the most careful arguments against religious belief that the Western tradition has produced.
questions: Hume argues that we cannot rationally justify inductive inference — our belief that the future will resemble the past. But science is built on inductive inference. If Hume is right, what follows for science? Can we have scientific knowledge without being able to justify induction?

Hume argues that reason cannot motivate action — that 'reason is the slave of the passions,' and only passions (desires, emotions) can move us to act. What follows for moral philosophy? If reason cannot motivate, what is the role of moral reasoning in ethics?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The key scholarly works are Norman Kemp Smith's The Philosophy of David Hume (1941 — the foundational naturalistic reading), Barry Stroud's Hume (1977 — the definitive analytic skeptical reading), Annette Baier's A Progress of Sentiments (1991 — the ethical and psychological reading), and Don Garrett's Cognition and Commitment in Hume's Philosophy (1997 — the most careful recent account of Hume's epistemology). Simon Blackburn's work on quasi-realism extends Hume's ethical sentimentalism.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Hume's philosophical arguments are the evidence — he is primarily a philosophical rather than historical figure, and the interpretation of his arguments is the primary scholarly activity. The limits: Hume's rhetoric is ironic and layered; his true views on religion and causation are not always transparent; and his two main works (Treatise and Enquiries) do not always agree.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Hume must engage with both the destructive (skeptical) and constructive (naturalist) dimensions of his work. Arguments that focus only on the skepticism miss the positive project; arguments that focus only on the naturalism must explain why the skeptical arguments are not devastating to it. The relationship between the problem of induction and scientific knowledge is the clearest case where Hume's challenge cannot be easily dismissed.
questions: Kant claimed that Hume woke him from his 'dogmatic slumber' and that his Critique of Pure Reason was a response to Hume. Is the Kantian response to Hume's challenge successful? Does Kant's account of the categories of understanding actually solve the problem of induction, or does it relocate it?

Hume argued that religious belief cannot be rationally justified — that testimony for miracles can never outweigh the evidence from the regularity of nature. Is this argument successful? Are there forms of religious belief that do not depend on the evidential claims Hume attacks?

research.topic: The problem of induction as an unsolved philosophical problem
research.content: Philosophical consensus has established that Hume's problem of induction — why we are rationally justified in expecting the future to resemble the past — has not been solved. Popper's falsificationism sidesteps rather than solves the problem. The pragmatist response (induction is justified because it works) is circular. The Bayesian response (induction is rational given prior probabilities) requires justifying the priors. The problem remains one of the most important unsolved problems in epistemology, and its implications for the rational foundations of science are not fully worked out.

research.topic: Hume's positive influence on Adam Smith and Scottish political economy
research.content: Historical scholarship has established the closeness of Hume and Smith's intellectual relationship and the extent to which Smith's moral philosophy and economics built on Humean foundations. Smith's concept of the 'impartial spectator' as the mechanism of moral judgment derives from Hume's account of moral sentiment. The methodology of The Wealth of Nations — observational, inductive, skeptical of a priori reasoning — reflects Humean influences. Emma Rothschild and Nicholas Phillipson have documented this relationship in detail.

Whether Hume was an atheist or an agnostic cannot be established with certainty from available evidence. He published his views carefully, often attributing the strongest skeptical positions to a character (Philo) in a dialogue rather than affirming them in his own name. His private correspondence and the accounts of his friends (Adam Smith, James Boswell) show a man who died calmly without religious conversion and who expressed skepticism about survival after death. What cannot be recovered: whether his private beliefs included any form of theism or whether his careful public presentation concealed genuine irreligion.

comparativeNarrative.name: Religious Philosophical Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: From within the religious philosophical tradition, Hume's arguments are responses that require engagement — his critique of the design argument, his analysis of miracles, and his account of the origins of religious belief in fear and anthropomorphism all demand serious responses. The natural theology tradition (from Aquinas through Swinburne) has provided those responses. Critics argue that Hume's 'Of Miracles' assumes what it proves (that miracles are antecedently improbable) and that his design argument critique was refuted by the development of fine-tuning arguments in contemporary cosmology.

comparativeNarrative.name: Continental Philosophical Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The continental tradition's reception of Hume has been mediated primarily through Kant's response. The subsequent development of German Idealism, phenomenology, and hermeneutics represents a different engagement with the Kantian response to Hume rather than direct engagement with Hume himself. Hegel's reading of Hume — as a necessary but insufficient moment in the dialectic of knowledge — places him within a progressive history of philosophy that Hume himself would have found implausible. The analytic-continental divide in philosophy partly reflects different readings of the Hume-Kant exchange.

comparativeNarrative.name: Scientific Practice Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: Working scientists rarely concern themselves with the problem of induction — they proceed on the assumption that inductive inference is reliable, because it works. From a practical scientific perspective, Hume's challenge is a philosopher's problem that does not affect actual scientific practice. The epistemological difficulty of justifying induction does not make physics or biology less reliable. The philosophical response — that this answer misses the point — does not change scientific practice, which suggests either that the problem is genuinely not important or that scientists have developed a kind of philosophical immunity to it.

rabbitHole.label: Kant
rabbitHole.reason: Kant's Critique of Pure Reason was explicitly a response to Hume — 'waking from dogmatic slumber' is Kant's description of reading Hume. Understanding Hume is essential for understanding what Kant was responding to.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: Hume is the Enlightenment's most rigorous practitioner and most uncomfortable critic — he took its commitment to reason and experience more seriously than anyone else and found consequences the Enlightenment did not want.

rabbitHole.label: The Scientific Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Hume's philosophical project was to do for the science of human nature what Newton had done for physical science — and his problem of induction is the deepest challenge to the rational foundations of Newtonian science.

rabbitHole.label: Probability
rabbitHole.reason: Hume's analysis of causation and induction connects directly to the philosophy of probability — both are attempts to understand the relationship between past experience and rational expectation about the future.

rabbitHole.label: Free Will
rabbitHole.reason: Hume's account of action as caused by desires and passions, not reason, bears directly on the free will debate — his compatibilism (that free will is compatible with causal determinism) is among the most influential in the tradition.

reference.author: Hume, David
reference.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
reference.annotation: The essential primary text — the Enquiry is the more polished and accessible presentation of the arguments of the Treatise, and the best starting point for Hume's epistemology and his analysis of causation, miracles, and the limits of reason.

reference.author: Hume, David
reference.title: Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
reference.annotation: The most careful philosophical analysis of religious belief — Hume's Dialogues present the most rigorous dissection of the design argument for God's existence in the Western tradition, and remain essential for philosophy of religion.

reference.author: Stroud, Barry
reference.title: Hume
reference.annotation: The definitive analytic account — Stroud's clear and rigorous analysis of Hume's arguments is the essential secondary source for the epistemological core of Hume's philosophy.

reference.author: Baier, Annette
reference.title: A Progress of Sentiments: Reflections on Hume's Treatise
reference.annotation: The essential ethical reading — Baier's account of Hume's Treatise as a unified work centered on moral psychology and the 'calm passions' provides the most influential feminist and sentimentalist reading.

reference.author: Kemp Smith, Norman
reference.title: The Philosophy of David Hume
reference.annotation: The foundational naturalistic reading — Kemp Smith's argument that Hume's primary aim was naturalistic rather than skeptical transformed twentieth-century Hume scholarship and remains the essential historical-philosophical account.

reference.author: Garrett, Don
reference.title: Cognition and Commitment in Hume's Philosophy
reference.annotation: The most careful recent account of Hume's epistemology — Garrett's analysis of Hume's cognitive psychology and its relationship to his skeptical arguments is essential for the current state of Hume scholarship.

commerce.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
commerce.author: David Hume
commerce.note: Start with the primary source — the Enquiry is shorter and more polished than the Treatise, and Hume's arguments are clear enough that secondary literature can wait until you have read him.

commerce.title: Hume: A Very Short Introduction
commerce.author: A.J. Ayer
commerce.note: The most accessible secondary introduction — Ayer's short account captures Hume's central arguments clearly and explains why they matter.

commerce.title: Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
commerce.author: David Hume
commerce.note: The essential text on religion — Hume's careful dissection of the design argument remains the most philosophically serious challenge to natural theology.

commerce.title: Hume
commerce.author: Barry Stroud
commerce.note: The best rigorous philosophical account — Stroud's clear analysis of Hume's arguments is the essential secondary source.

commerce.title: A Progress of Sentiments
commerce.author: Annette Baier
commerce.note: The essential ethical and psychological reading — Baier's account of the Treatise as a unified work centered on moral psychology is indispensable.

commerce.title: The Philosophy of David Hume
commerce.author: Norman Kemp Smith
commerce.note: The foundational historical account — Kemp Smith's naturalistic reading transformed Hume scholarship and remains the essential historical-philosophical account.

popularCulture.title: 'Correlation is not causation' as Humean legacy
popularCulture.description: The statistical principle that correlation does not imply causation — now a staple of scientific literacy and internet argumentation — is a popularization of Hume's insight that we cannot observe causal connections, only regular successions. When scientists distinguish correlation from causation in epidemiology, when fact-checkers invoke it against misleading statistics, they are applying Hume's analysis. The principle is one of the most widely used pieces of philosophical reasoning in everyday life.

popularCulture.title: The is-ought gap in contemporary ethics and politics
popularCulture.description: Hume's observation that you cannot derive 'ought' from 'is' — that normative conclusions do not follow from descriptive premises — appears repeatedly in political debate. Appeals to nature, to tradition, or to evolution ('humans evolved to do X, therefore X is right') all commit the fallacy Hume identified. The is-ought gap is one of the most practically important philosophical principles, yet it is regularly violated in political argument across the spectrum.

popularCulture.title: The self as bundle of perceptions in cognitive science
popularCulture.description: Hume's bundle theory of the self — there is no continuous entity, only a rapid succession of perceptions — has found support in cognitive science and Buddhist philosophy. Neuroscience of self (Damasio, Metzinger) suggests the sense of being a unified self is a construction rather than a basic fact. The convergence of analytic philosophy, Buddhist thought, and neuroscience on this point is one of the more striking examples of cross-cultural philosophical agreement.

popularCulture.title: The miracle problem and contemporary religious epistemology
popularCulture.description: The debate about Hume's essay 'Of Miracles' — arguing that testimony for miracles can never outweigh the evidence from nature's regularity — remains live in contemporary philosophy of religion. Philosophers like Swinburne and Alston have argued Hume's argument is circular; others like Earman argue it fails on its own probabilistic terms. The miracle debate is one of the most active intersections of analytic philosophy and philosophy of religion.

popularCulture.title: Hume's calm death as philosophical exemplum
popularCulture.description: Hume's death in 1776 — calm, cheerful, non-religious — was witnessed and reported by Adam Smith and James Boswell, whose accounts circulated widely. For believers, the equanimity of a non-believer in the face of death was disturbing and required explanation. For secularists and atheists, Hume's death became an exemplum: evidence that one can live and die well without religious consolation. The death is still cited in philosophical discussions of secular mortality.
